etymology.description: 猴(猴) (hóu, "monkey") + 子(子) (zi, "child; small thing") forms "monkey"; here 子(子) (zi, "child") functions as a suffix indicating a small or familiar object/creature.
etymology.interpretation: 猴(猴) (hóu, "monkey") represents the main concept of the animal. The addition of 子(子) (zi, "child") acts as a diminutive or familiarizing suffix, commonly used in Chinese to indicate a small or endearing form of the noun it follows. This combination suggests a small or familiar version of the animal, akin to how "monkey" is understood in English. The use of 子(子) reflects a linguistic pattern common in Chinese to create affectionate or casual noun forms.

examples.chinese: 猴子在树上跳来跳去(猴子在樹上跳來跳去)。
examples.pinyin: Hóuzi zài shù shàng tiào lái tiào qù.
examples.english: The monkey is jumping around in the tree.

examples.chinese: 我们在动物园看到了一只猴子(我們在動物園看到了一隻猴子)。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen zài dòngwùyuán kàn dàole yī zhī hóuzi.
examples.english: We saw a monkey at the zoo.

examples.chinese: 猴子的动作非常敏捷(猴子的動作非常敏捷)。
examples.english: The monkey's movements are very agile.
